author: @alanboltz3405
text: I have used one or both of two methods. I first try a tad bit of acetone. Acetone puts micro abrasions on the surface of the plastic and usually works quite well. This is what we call in woodworking as de-glazing the surface. Often times the second method I use is a fine grit steel wool. This will physically de-glaze the surface of the plastic and allow the ink to adhere. Voila!

author: @Hobypyrocom
text: what's the difference between milled graphene (as in that ink) and milled graphite? isn't graphite just graphene sheets stacked? so what is the difference when they are powdered?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: about 100,000 layers

replies.author: @Hobypyrocom
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering yeah but there is no much difference between graphite and graphene when they are in powder form...
if i had some graphene powder i would be able to prove my point on my microscope... i can make a video about graphite powder under microscope and you will be able to see what i am talking about...

replies.author: @Rollmops94
replies.text: @Hobypyrocom There is a lot of difference in the way they behave.

replies.author: @Hobypyrocom
replies.text: @Rollmops94  i guess you are right, but where does the difference come from? because graphite is just graphene sheets stacked above each other, if you mill graphite to a really fine powder, those graphene sheets will separate... i have a ball mill, i have a microscope and i have graphite, i will make a test and make a video of it once i have time just to see the outcome.

replies.author: @Rollmops94
replies.text:
@ Well. If we assume graphite is nothing but stacked graphene it has to be that stacking that leads to it being less conductive. Honestly I don‘t understand why graphite does not unstack and/or graphene does not restack. Maybe they do. From what I understand graphene only has amazing conductance within a single layer. I‘d assume that you‘d break these layers up when you powder them, so it is questionable wether powdered graphene has these amazing properties at all. But maybe not even that is true since graphene layers are supposedly very strong. Maybe they just fold.
I can‘t find a lot of info on the subject. I also feel like this channel is not the most scientific.
I can‘t even find easy info on different conductive paints and how conductive they are. A lot of people seem to use graphite based paints. What about metals like silver and copper? People rarely seem to mix all of those. What about the binder? Wouldn‘t the best conductive paint have no binders since binders don‘t conduct? Couldn‘t you get mechanical strength by pressing the material after the paint dried? How do they do graphite rods? Aren‘t they just pressed or do they add binder as well? I could ask questions like this for days.
